name: Japan
tagline: Imperial myth, borrowed continental learning, and centuries of warrior rule braided into a singular modern state.
summary: Japanese history unfolds across more than two millennia on an archipelago off the eastern edge of Asia. From the rice-farming Yayoi settlements emerged the Yamato state, whose chieftains claimed descent from the sun goddess and whose imperial line, by tradition, has never been broken. Successive ages absorbed Chinese script, Buddhism, and statecraft, then refined them into something unmistakably Japanese: the courtly elegance of Heian Kyoto, the warrior ethos of the samurai, the iron stability of the Tokugawa peace. After more than two centuries of self-imposed isolation, Japan responded to Western pressure not with collapse but with the Meiji Restoration, racing to build a modern industrial empire. Imperial overreach ended in the catastrophe of 1945, after which a reconstructed, constitutionally pacifist Japan rose to become a democratic, economic power while its emperor endured as a symbol of the state.

founding.label: Legendary founding of the imperial line
founding.detail: Japanese tradition, recorded in the 8th-century chronicles Kojiki and Nihon shoki, dates the founding of the realm to the accession of the legendary Emperor Jimmu in 660 BCE, a descendant of the sun goddess Amaterasu. No historical evidence supports Jimmu's existence; modern scholarship treats him as a mythical figure and locates the real emergence of organised Japanese statehood in the Yamato polity of central Honshu around the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.

quickFacts.label: Traditional founding
quickFacts.value: 660 BCE, by the legendary Emperor Jimmu (mythical)

quickFacts.label: Imperial dynasty
quickFacts.value: The world's oldest continuous hereditary monarchy by tradition

quickFacts.label: Indigenous religion
quickFacts.value: Shinto, coexisting with Buddhism since the 6th century CE

quickFacts.label: Writing system
quickFacts.value: Adapted from Chinese characters; kana scripts developed in the Heian period

quickFacts.label: Era of warrior rule
quickFacts.value: Roughly 1185 to 1868, governed by shoguns rather than emperors

quickFacts.label: Self-isolation (sakoku)
quickFacts.value: Closed to most foreign contact from the 1630s until 1853 to 1854

eras.title: Yamato and Asuka: The Birth of the State
eras.standfirst: Out of the rice paddies and burial mounds of central Honshu rose the Yamato court, which imported Buddhism, the Chinese writing system, and continental statecraft to forge Japan's first centralised government.
body: By the 3rd and 4th centuries CE, a dominant polity centered on the Yamato plain of central Honshu had begun to extend its authority over rival clans. Its rulers built the colossal keyhole-shaped kofun tomb mounds that give the era its archaeological name and claimed sacred descent from the sun goddess Amaterasu, a lineage that would underpin imperial legitimacy for all of subsequent Japanese history. The mythical narratives later compiled in the Kojiki and Nihon shoki projected this dynasty back to the legendary Emperor Jimmu in 660 BCE, but the verifiable state took shape only in these centuries.

The Asuka period, named for the region south of present-day Nara where the court resided, opened with the transformative arrival of Buddhism, traditionally dated to the mid-6th century via the Korean kingdom of Baekje. After fierce factional struggle, the powerful Soga clan secured the new religion's place at court. Under the regent Prince Shotoku, Japan deliberately remodeled itself on the Chinese pattern: he dispatched embassies to the Sui court, promoted Buddhism, and is credited with the Seventeen-Article Constitution of 604, a Confucian-Buddhist statement of governing ethics.

The momentum toward centralization culminated in the Taika Reform initiated in 645, which sought to reorganize land and population under direct imperial control on the Chinese model. Over the following decades these efforts hardened into the ritsuryo system of legal codes and bureaucratic government, laying the institutional foundation for the classical Japanese state that the Nara capital would soon embody.

events.title: Rise of the Yamato state
events.summary: Clan leaders on the Yamato plain consolidate power, build monumental kofun tombs, and establish the imperial lineage claiming descent from the sun goddess Amaterasu.

events.title: Introduction of Buddhism
events.summary: Buddhism reaches Japan from the Korean kingdom of Baekje, traditionally dated to 552; it is championed by the Soga clan over conservative opposition.

events.title: Prince Shotoku's Seventeen-Article Constitution
events.summary: As regent, Prince Shotoku promulgates a set of Confucian and Buddhist moral precepts for government and intensifies the adoption of Chinese institutions.

events.title: The Taika Reform
events.summary: A court coup destroys Soga dominance and inaugurates reforms reorganizing land and administration along centralized Chinese lines, the basis of the ritsuryo state.

figures.name: Prince Shotoku (Shotoku Taishi)
figures.blurb: Regent for Empress Suiko, he gave Buddhism official patronage, resumed embassies to China, and is traditionally credited with the Seventeen-Article Constitution and the founding of temples such as Horyu-ji, helping to define Japan's early state.

eras.title: Nara and Heian: The Classical Age
eras.standfirst: With permanent capitals at Nara and then Heian-kyo (Kyoto), an aristocratic court presided over a golden age of Buddhist art, vernacular literature, and Fujiwara political dominance.
body: In 710 the court established Japan's first enduring capital at Heijo-kyo (Nara), laid out on a grid modeled on the Tang Chinese capital of Chang'an. The Nara period saw the consolidation of Buddhist institutions as instruments of state, epitomized by the casting of the great bronze Buddha at Todai-ji, and the compilation of the foundational chronicles Kojiki and Nihon shoki along with the Man'yoshu poetry anthology. The growing wealth and political weight of the great monasteries, however, prompted the court to seek distance from them.

In 794 Emperor Kanmu moved the capital to Heian-kyo, modern Kyoto, inaugurating the Heian period, which lasted until 1185 and is regarded as the classical high point of Japanese aristocratic culture. The Chinese-style centralized order gradually loosened as tax-exempt private estates, the shoen, expanded at the expense of the public domain. Real power flowed to the Fujiwara family, who dominated the court for generations by marrying their daughters to emperors and ruling as regents.

Sheltered behind these political arrangements, the Heian aristocracy cultivated an exquisite court culture. The development of the kana syllabaries freed Japanese writing from exclusive reliance on Chinese characters and enabled a flowering of vernacular literature, above all Murasaki Shikibu's The Tale of Genji, often called the world's first novel. By the late Heian period, provincial warrior houses, the Taira and Minamoto, had grown powerful enough to intervene in court politics, and their rivalry would bring the classical age to a violent close.

events.title: Capital established at Nara
events.summary: The court founds Japan's first permanent capital, Heijo-kyo (Nara), modeled on the Tang Chinese capital, beginning the Nara period.

events.title: Capital moved to Heian-kyo (Kyoto)
events.summary: Emperor Kanmu relocates the capital to Heian-kyo, the site of Kyoto, opening the Heian period and a long era of aristocratic court culture.

events.title: The Tale of Genji
events.summary: The court lady Murasaki Shikibu completes The Tale of Genji, widely regarded as the world's first full-length novel and the summit of Heian literature.

events.title: Genpei War
events.summary: Civil war between the Taira and Minamoto warrior clans ends with Minamoto victory at Dan-no-ura in 1185, shattering court supremacy and ushering in warrior rule.

figures.name: Murasaki Shikibu
figures.blurb: A member of a lesser branch of the Fujiwara and lady-in-waiting at the Heian court, she wrote The Tale of Genji, a psychologically rich portrait of aristocratic life generally considered Japan's greatest literary work.

pullquote.text: The Tale of Genji is generally considered the greatest work of Japanese literature and thought to be the world's oldest full-length novel.

eras.title: Kamakura and Muromachi: The Age of the Shogun
eras.standfirst: Power passed from courtiers to warriors as Minamoto Yoritomo founded the shogunate, a system of military government that, with the Ashikaga that followed, would dominate Japan for centuries.
body: Victory in the Genpei War made Minamoto Yoritomo the supreme military authority in Japan. Establishing his government, the bakufu, at Kamakura, he was named shogun in 1192, inaugurating a dual structure in which the emperor reigned in Kyoto while the shogun ruled in fact. The Kamakura shogunate firmly entrenched feudal relations between warrior lords and their retainers and, after Yoritomo's death, real authority shifted to the Hojo regents. The regime's most celebrated achievement was repelling the Mongol invasions launched by Kublai Khan in 1274 and 1281, when storms remembered as the kamikaze, or divine winds, helped destroy the invading fleets.

The Kamakura shogunate fell in 1333, and after a brief imperial restoration Ashikaga Takauji established a new shogunate in 1338, ruling from the Muromachi district of Kyoto. The Muromachi period was politically turbulent, divided early on between rival northern and southern imperial courts, yet culturally luminous. Under Ashikaga patronage Zen Buddhism shaped an aesthetic of restraint that produced ink painting, the tea ceremony, Noh theater, and landmark architecture such as Kyoto's Golden and Silver Pavilions.

Ashikaga authority was always thinner than the Kamakura precedent, resting on coalitions of regional warlords. When the Onin War erupted in 1467, devastating Kyoto and fracturing central control, the shogunate became largely powerless. Japan dissolved into the warring-states condition that would define the following century, even as the formal Muromachi shogunate endured until 1573.

events.title: Minamoto Yoritomo becomes shogun
events.summary: Yoritomo is appointed shogun, formalizing the Kamakura shogunate and a system of warrior government that would last, in varying forms, until 1868.

events.title: Mongol invasions repelled
events.summary: Kublai Khan's Yuan forces twice attempt to invade Japan; both fleets are wrecked by typhoons later mythologized as the kamikaze, or divine winds.

events.title: Ashikaga Takauji founds the Muromachi shogunate
events.summary: Takauji establishes the second shogunate, ruling from Kyoto's Muromachi district and presiding over a brilliant if unstable cultural era.

events.title: Onin War
events.summary: A succession dispute ignites a decade of warfare that devastates Kyoto, cripples shogunal authority, and tips Japan into the fragmented Sengoku era.

figures.name: Minamoto Yoritomo
figures.blurb: After leading the Minamoto to victory in the Genpei War, he founded the bakufu at Kamakura and took the title of shogun, establishing the warrior government that would rule Japan for roughly seven centuries.

eras.title: Sengoku and Edo: Unification and the Long Peace
eras.standfirst: A century of civil war ended when three great unifiers reassembled the realm; the Tokugawa then imposed more than two and a half centuries of internal peace, social order, and self-imposed isolation from the wider world.
body: From the late 15th century Japan was riven by the Sengoku, or warring-states, period, in which independent daimyo fought for territory amid the ruins of central authority. The introduction of European firearms and the arrival of Portuguese traders and Jesuit missionaries from the 1540s added new forces to the conflict. Out of this chaos came the three unifiers: Oda Nobunaga, who broke the power of rival warlords and militant Buddhist orders; Toyotomi Hideyoshi, a commoner who completed the unification of the provinces by 1590 and launched ill-fated invasions of Korea; and finally Tokugawa Ieyasu.

Ieyasu's decisive victory at the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600 left him master of Japan, and in 1603 he was named shogun, founding the Tokugawa shogunate based at Edo (modern Tokyo). The Tokugawa regime built a durable order: a rigid status hierarchy of samurai, peasants, artisans, and merchants; the alternate-attendance system that bound the daimyo to the shogun; and a guarded reverence for the still-powerless imperial court at Kyoto.

Fearing the destabilizing effect of Christianity and foreign intrigue, the shogunate enforced the sakoku policy from the 1630s, expelling missionaries, banning Japanese from going abroad, and confining foreign trade largely to Dutch and Chinese merchants at Nagasaki. The result was more than two centuries of near-total peace, during which a vibrant urban and merchant culture flourished, producing kabuki theater, ukiyo-e woodblock prints, and haiku poetry. This stability held until 1853, when the arrival of Commodore Matthew Perry's American squadron forced Japan open and set in motion the shogunate's collapse.

events.title: Arrival of Europeans and firearms
events.summary: Portuguese traders reach Japan, introducing firearms and, soon after, Christianity, adding new pressures to the Sengoku wars.

events.title: Hideyoshi completes unification
events.summary: Toyotomi Hideyoshi brings the last domains under central control, finishing the reunification of Japan begun by Oda Nobunaga.

events.title: Battle of Sekigahara
events.summary: Tokugawa Ieyasu defeats a rival coalition in the decisive battle that makes him master of Japan and clears the path to the shogunate.

events.title: Tokugawa shogunate founded
events.summary: Ieyasu is appointed shogun and establishes his government at Edo, beginning the Tokugawa (Edo) period of long internal peace.

events.title: Sakoku: national seclusion
events.summary: A series of edicts seals Japan from most foreign contact, banning Christianity and overseas travel and restricting trade to Nagasaki for over two centuries.

figures.name: Tokugawa Ieyasu
figures.blurb: The last of the three great unifiers, he won supremacy at Sekigahara in 1600 and became shogun in 1603, founding a dynasty that ruled Japan in peace from Edo for more than 250 years.

figures.name: Toyotomi Hideyoshi
figures.blurb: Rising from peasant origins to chief minister, he completed Japan's unification by 1590, conducted sweeping land surveys and disarmament of the peasantry, and launched two costly invasions of Korea.

pullquote.text: Central control was established through the efforts of the three great unifiers, who laid the foundations for the long-lasting Tokugawa period.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the Sengoku period

eras.title: Meiji and Empire: Revolution from Above
eras.standfirst: Confronted by Western power, Japan toppled the shogunate, restored the emperor, and remade itself into a modern industrial nation-state, then a colonial empire whose ambitions led to catastrophic war.
body: The forced opening of Japan exposed the Tokugawa order as unable to defend the country, and reformist samurai rallied behind the slogan of restoring imperial rule. In a coup in Kyoto in January 1868, they proclaimed the young Emperor Meiji ruler in his own right and abolished the shogunate. The Meiji Restoration was less a return to the past than a revolution from above: over the following decades the leadership dismantled the feudal domains, abolished the legal privileges of the samurai class, and introduced conscription, compulsory education, a national tax system, and a Western-style constitution in 1889.

Japan industrialized with extraordinary speed, building railways, factories, a modern army and navy, and the institutions of a centralized state. Determined to win recognition as a great power and to avoid the colonial subjugation suffered elsewhere in Asia, Japan also turned outward. Victory in the First Sino-Japanese War of 1894-1895 and, more startlingly, in the Russo-Japanese War of 1904-1905 announced its arrival as an imperial power, and it annexed Korea in 1910.

In the 1930s, economic strain and an increasingly autonomous military pushed Japan toward expansionist aggression, beginning with the seizure of Manchuria in 1931 and full-scale war with China from 1937. Allied with Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy, Japan attacked Pearl Harbor in December 1941, opening the Pacific War. After early conquests across Southeast Asia and the Pacific, the tide turned, and the war ended in 1945 with American firebombing of Japanese cities, the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, the Soviet entry into the war, and Japan's surrender.

events.title: Meiji Restoration
events.summary: A coup ends the Tokugawa shogunate and restores direct imperial rule under Emperor Meiji, launching rapid modernization and Westernization.

events.title: Meiji Constitution promulgated
events.summary: Japan adopts a Western-influenced constitution establishing a constitutional monarchy with an imperial parliament, the Diet.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War
events.summary: Japan's victory over Russia marks the first modern defeat of a European power by an Asian state and confirms Japan as a major power.

events.title: Attack on Pearl Harbor
events.summary: Japan attacks the U.S. fleet at Pearl Harbor, bringing the United States into World War II and opening the Pacific War.

events.title: Atomic bombings and surrender
events.summary: The United States drops atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki on August 6 and 9; Japan announces surrender, formalized on September 2, ending World War II.

figures.name: Emperor Meiji (Mutsuhito)
figures.blurb: In whose name the 1868 restoration was carried out, he reigned over Japan's transformation from a secluded feudal society into a modern industrial and military power and the framing of its first constitution.

pullquote.text: The Meiji Restoration of 1868 came to be identified with the subsequent era of major political, economic, and social change that brought about the modernization and Westernization of the country.

eras.title: Postwar Japan: Reconstruction and the Modern Nation
eras.standfirst: Defeated and occupied, Japan adopted a pacifist democratic constitution, regained sovereignty, and rose through an extraordinary economic miracle to become one of the world's leading industrial democracies.
body: From 1945 to 1952 Japan was occupied by the Allies under General Douglas MacArthur, who oversaw sweeping reforms: the dismantling of the military, land redistribution, the breakup of the great industrial combines, women's suffrage, and the trial of war leaders. The centerpiece was a new constitution that took effect in 1947, transforming the emperor into a symbol of the state, establishing popular sovereignty and a parliamentary democracy, and renouncing war and the maintenance of war potential in its famous Article 9.

Japan regained full sovereignty with the Treaty of San Francisco, signed in 1951 and effective in 1952, which ended the occupation while binding Japan closely to the United States through a security alliance. Freed to concentrate on reconstruction, Japan experienced a postwar economic miracle: decades of rapid growth that, by the 1960s and 1970s, made it one of the largest economies in the world, a leader in automobiles, electronics, and advanced manufacturing.

The collapse of an asset price bubble in the early 1990s ushered in a prolonged period of slow growth and deflation. In the 21st century Japan has continued as a stable, affluent democracy and a major global economy, while contending with an aging and shrinking population and recurring natural disasters, including the 2011 Tohoku earthquake, tsunami, and Fukushima nuclear accident. Its imperial institution endures: in 2019 Emperor Akihito abdicated in favor of his son Naruhito, opening the Reiwa era.

events.title: Pacifist constitution takes effect
events.summary: Japan's postwar constitution establishes democracy and popular sovereignty, makes the emperor a symbol of the state, and renounces war under Article 9.

events.title: Treaty of San Francisco and end of occupation
events.summary: Signed in 1951 and effective in 1952, the peace treaty restores Japanese sovereignty and ends the Allied occupation.

events.title: Postwar economic miracle
events.summary: Hosting the Tokyo Olympics symbolizes Japan's recovery as it becomes an industrial powerhouse during decades of rapid, sustained growth.

events.title: Tohoku earthquake and Fukushima disaster
events.summary: A magnitude-9.0 earthquake and tsunami devastate northeastern Japan and trigger meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant.

figures.name: Douglas MacArthur
figures.role: Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers
figures.blurb: As head of the Allied occupation from 1945, the American general directed the demilitarization and democratic reform of Japan, including the drafting of its 1947 constitution.

figures.name: Emperor Jimmu
figures.blurb: The mythical founder of the imperial dynasty, said to descend from the sun goddess Amaterasu. No evidence supports his existence; historians regard him as a legendary embodiment of the rise of the Yamato state.

figures.name: Oda Nobunaga
figures.role: First of the three great unifiers
figures.blurb: A ruthless and innovative daimyo who broke the power of rival warlords and militant Buddhist establishments, beginning the reunification of Japan before his death by betrayal in 1582.

sources.label: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
Occupation and Reconstruction of Japan, 1945-52
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
